original_abstract: This paper examines the complex landscape of internationalization in higher education, focusing on emergent tensions between the tradition of education as a public good and its growing status as a commercial export. Through an analysis of the General Agreement on Trade in Services (GATS) and regional case studies—most notably Australia’s heavy reliance on international student revenue—the text explores how "cross-border" education impacts equity, access, and cultural diversity. While market-driven expansion has provided vital subsidies for public institutions, it has also led to concerns regarding the homogenization of curricula, the erosion of domestic quality, and the rise of a "for-profit" sector that often blurs the lines between academic scholarship and vocational training. Ultimately, the paper argues that as international higher education matures, stakeholders must adopt a more critical proactive stance to ensure that the drive for innovation and revenue does not undermine the fundamental quality and cultural integrity of global academic systems.
humanized_abstract: This paper describes the current state of internationalisation in higher education and the contradictions it generates, particularly the increasing conflict between the traditions of internationalisation based on the values of education and the increasing marketisation of a commodity which can be sold as a global service. Using the General Agreement on Trade in Services (GATS) and citing examples at the regional level (particularly the case of Australia where the tertiary education sector derives a large income from fees paid by international students), the paper shows how cross-border education has profound effects on equity, access, diversity and quality. On the one hand, the economic benefits deriving from the world-wide sales of study programmes are used to support the public access to higher education. On the other hand, a whole range of problems have been observed such as uniformisation of the curricula at the world level and the risk of a fall in the quality of the education provided in the host country, the commercialisation of tertiary education with the emergence of a for profit sector that can no longer see itself as part of the scientific community and the increasing reduction of academic education to a purely vocational and quantitative training. The conclusions of this paper are that it is essential for education stakeholders to take a more sophisticated and more active attitude towards this evolving internationalisation of learning because, once the world-wide commercialisation of education has taken place, all the necessary measures to respect the balance of the quality of the system that develops with the growth of new international activities will be too late.
